sentences.text: Thank you.
Thank you, too.
The thing that I know is that crematorium 2, where I worked, was the biggest one. 1, 3, and 4 were smaller one, and were about a mile away from us.
As far as I know, from the beginning, cremat-- I was in crematorium 2.
And that all the time they were talking about crematorium 2.
I didn't know anything else.
Yes.
Yeah, we were just on the ramp on the Birkenau.
Yeah.
And what is the other question?

The procedure was that they were just taking numbers where you would be, you know, actually, and most of them were signalizing that 1-- 10 times this, 1 times this, you know. 10 times that would be to go direct to-- to-- through the gas chambers, and the others to work.
And always came a number of 10% of the overall for working, and the balance, an hour later, they didn't exist anymore.
Yeah, I-- I think most of the time I thought was Mengele at-- that was doing the selections.
I don't know if it was all the time, but he's one of the principals, you know, doing the selections.
I think it was Mengele carrying the selection at that time, yeah.
Yeah, it's 11 days after the [? courthouse. ?] I think-- was a-- was written down.
I don't remember, was the 14th, or--
11 of April, yeah.
The only thing that I know is after you are in the ramp, they come in and they process you, you know.
I don't remember nothing else.
Because after that, when they take you away, you know, you are already in quarantine.
Not exactly, but I was in the quarantine for a while, you know.
And let me see if I can remember.

[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] When they took us out of the camp to go to the quarantine on this [? place, ?] they would-- would put the number with a pen, with a pen that wrote-- actually was putting down [INAUDIBLE] writing the numbers.
And then afterward they finish.
At the end, I had a-- I know was swollen for 20 days before it came back to normal.
Not before, but after, you know.
During the quarantine, you got the number.
Well, it was full of my family, [?
Mores ?] Venezia family-- and [?
Mores, ?] Venezia, and Shlomo, you know.
These are the three that I remember being with us.
And we were in the same one, [INAUDIBLE].
I-- I knew them, yes.
Oh, sure.
That there was a long process, was very, very good when you have people who around you, you know, because you don't know where you're going.
We [INAUDIBLE] know that we will be killed right away.
So that was very good news when you had people around you looking at each other.
By talking.
The only thing I can remember is, you know, don't stop talking.
Even if you don't know anything, you try to have a conversation of any kind to be able to forget what the-- the balance of your voyage will be.
Well, no, the situation was very, very intense, when-- to find out-- because everybody was thinking what [INAUDIBLE] are going to be happening, because we didn't know exactly.
Once we don't know what our faith will be, always you make assumptions of kinds, that we'll be OK, we will not be OK.
We are going to do this, we are going to do that.
But nobody knew that we are going to die.
The conversation-- mostly the conversation we could talk about and would be, where are we going?
We're taking time, days and days and days.
And we were figuring out-- I was figuring out, what these people want to do with us.
So doesn't-- nothing comes out until you are there.
The destination, I don't-- I was [INAUDIBLE] my conclusion that won't be good enough, you know, from seeing what is going on.
Because the-- the SS were-- I will say, people that they want to have fights, confusion, you know, with everybody.
And-- and we weren't ready for that.
We were finding out not too long after what was going on, because people already knew around.
And if you see the documentary of ours, you see when-- when Shlomo was asking somebody.
And they were showing to him to see up there, you know, what was going on, you know.
So we knew that the outcome won't be good.
Well, I didn't know at that time it was a mass killing.
Didn't take too long, because I-- when they selected my-- my brother, the small brother [INAUDIBLE], seeing my mother and father going into the bus, the truck, whatever it was, he ran with them.
And then after a day or two I found out that there is no coming back.
So we knew very fast what was going on.
And there were always some people, that they knew exactly that it was a killing around going on.
Well, many times we knew that our days were numbered, especially after we went to the sonderkommando, you know.
On that-- every the six months of whatever, they used to change them, you know.
I was lucky that I was on the last one.
But before, every six months they would change the sonderkommando with new ones, because always there were people coming in for the same purpose, you know.
But you know, always you go-- I used to go by, if I am alive the next day.
That-- that was always my-- my vision, was not to go in front of the Germans, always, because I made up my mind that that war will be ending one day.
And I want to be-- if I'm alive, I want to be the guy to tell the story.
Well, very soon, you know, because I knew that, you know, I didn't have too much time.
So I have to find ways to see.
You know, I was always observing what was happening.
Every day, you know, our-- the Germans were really some-- most of them were very tough people, you know.
They didn't want us around.
We didn't have too much to do with them.
We were only 30 days of-- and then after the 30 days, we were already in the sonderkommando.
Yes.
That was-- that was a selection.
They selected, I think at that time, if I remember, they selected 35 Greeks to go into the sonderkommando.
That's all I know.
Well, yeah, they came one day, and they-- we didn't know.
They selected a new one to block 13.
We knew that block 13 was the sonderkommando.
Physically?
Well, it was part of the camp.
I, you know, I don't know exactly the route or where exactly.
But it wasn't too far away, you know.
It just.
We didn't-- they didn't let us know too much about.
We knew that we had to work day and night, our 12-hour shift.
And you know, when you had that in mind-- and they were always, go, fast, schnell, or whatever it was.
We know that-- we knew that was the motto of these people.
They wanted to have as much as they could to kill as fast as they-- they can have it.
It-- it was become a daily routine for us.
And every day was something coming, you know.
At that time, it was during the end of the-- of Europe, you know.
And when we went in '44, they already killed probably most of them.
The biggest one was the Hungarian Jews coming.
These were the big one.
It's I don't remember the day, but it wasn't too far after 30 days of quarantine.
They-- they sent us to-- they did whatever they had to do.
Well, I remember that we had a-- a garden outside on the sonderkommando, I think.
That-- that what I remember.
It was nice.
They kept it very, very nice all around.
I-- I think it was a fence.
Nobody could come close.
So.
Yeah.
I-- I don't really remember.
I know it was down.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we couldn't even talk to anyone when they were getting undressed, you know.
The SS were very tough.
And we-- we couldn't give anybody any suggestions, or tell them what would happen.

But-- but I know were up, where they were throwing the gas, just was the German with a couple of people of-- of us, and to open up the 18-inch whatever it was, or the opening to throw the gas was very heavy.
And so the Germans used to do it themself, throw in the gas.
Well, the day-- the day begins, you know, just-- you get up.

And they give you-- the-- the thing is that the-- the sonderkommando, you know, they had always better delivery of-- of things than the regular ones, because everybody coming, always they brought a few cases or a few boxes of food.
And the Germans, they didn't care.
You can have anything you want, and anything, undressing like this.
So we had that.
That was OK.
But inside of us wasn't OK, you know.
I couldn't stomach, you know.
They give us-- it will be better to have the soup and die before time.
But seeing that today, for me was better that that I was this way, so I can-- I can--
The living accommodations were better than everybody else.
Was on top of the crematorium-- regular bed, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I don't know, what else-- what else can we ask for?
Well, I don't remember how many of us.
But it was a full-- a full people that were doing the business, doing the work.
I don't know, maybe.
But it was, as far as I know it so many beds, so whatever they had to do.
Maybe we-- we slept with-- somebody slept in my bed and I slept in their bed.
But-- but it was this, at that time, was the only thing that we were looking-- we were looking where we were better than the other people.
And they're going-- but we were better in a certain way, you know.
If you sit down and think what we were doing, you know, seeing so many cadavers, you know, go and pick them up, taking them out, put them in the thing, put them in-- to go to the ovens.
Everybody-- you know, our minds were very confused.
There is always an appell, you know.
And I think everything was outside the appell, you know, if it's cold and everything.
But they would continue every day, say, if you are there.
It was early, you know, 7.
I don't know.
I never thought about these things.
Oh, yeah.
You had your own functions, you know.
They-- most of-- of us were taking from the gas chambers, taking them out, and put them in a wagon, put them in the elevator that went to the second floor.
And the-- the second floor was somebody else putting them in the oven.
[CLEARING THROAT]
Oh, yeah, yeah.
You will know that it's coming, 2,500 people, or 1,500, or 2,000.
Most of the time were from 15 to 2,500.
The big ones were when the-- the Hungarian came in, then because they wanted to get rid of them fast enough.
And they only way they could realize is to bring 15,000 a day.
It was something that I couldn't believe.
Another capo will announce, you know.
We didn't have any to do with the Germans.
Everything was the inside site of, you know.
We had only about two Germans outside.
That's all.
Well, they knew what time were coming and we were ready in the undressing room, and wherever we had to do.
And going, actually, we were very, you know, to see that everybody goes into the gas chamber, too.
No, you cannot tell anything to anyone.
Just undress fast enough.
And you were-- you were there to help them undress, to get undressed.
They didn't take too long to undress.
And then they are getting in line and going to-- when you get into the gas chamber, then a lot of people, they are not all stupid, you know.
They-- they were seeing what it was, that no-- no water coming or anything.
So it took a while.
But after they closed the door, they knew that that was the end of them.
Well, everybody-- the atmosphere was very tense.
The people, they couldn't-- you couldn't tell them what was going to happen, but they were thinking themselves.
And I think they were thinking that-- that their lives were ending, you know.
So you could go there in the crematorium, you [INAUDIBLE] is the end of you.
The-- we were telling them.
There were a couple of Germans there, but we were the ones to tell undress, take out everything, you know.
And you know, also, people were very distressed to find they were nude, you know, especially the women.
They were confused.
And I observed, and they were confused.
And you couldn't tell them anything.
No, I didn't-- I did not-- I didn't see anyone.
The only thing I saw sometimes were some friends of ours that they came in.
And there were also Shlomo, a couple of-- they were relatives.
They came in one time, and I-- I told them, look, you better go there.
That is taking two minutes and you die.
If you go there, it would be five minutes, and you'll suffer, you know.
At that time, we told them that there is no way.
And one of them asked Shlomo, why don't you tell the German.
You know, maybe he can relieve him, leave him alone.
And Shlomo, poor guy, said, I don't think I can do anything, but I'll talk to him.
And he talked to the guy.
And he told him, oh, you shit, something like that, you know, in German.
Everything goes through.
Oh, yeah.
No, he-- he-- he was just looking, you know.
The orders were given by the capos.
If they have any problems, probably he will come in, you know.
There was sometimes happening then.
You know.
Most of them, their inside, you know, everybody is confused.
You come to someplace that you don't know what will happen to you.
They're trying to find comfort.
And how do you find the comfort?
There's no such a thing.
And you cannot talk to them.
We were always, you know, down.
We didn't-- we knew that our days are numbered somehow.
But we had to do-- you know, everybody's complaining about our jobs, you know.
All the-- if you can talk to survivors, they always will be against us.
They tell you-- they don't tell you much.
Go into a line, and they start bringing them in.
That didn't take too long, actually, when they-- but first of all, they used to put in, instead of 12 or 1,400, up to 2,000, you know, like sardines.
Then they knew that something was wrong when they closed the door, because afterwards, even if you are around there, you see the scratching of the people on the walls.
And you know, when they finish you see a lot of blood.
That's why you had to paint it again.
Yeah.
If they-- if there's not enough room, you used to push.
I don't-- I don't know.
But when you are like that, you know, you see that your mother or your son-- or your-- you go in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, there are always people against things that they are not natural.
The only thing, [INAUDIBLE] you push them in.
This is nothing.
We would push them in.
The SS, they don't get [INAUDIBLE].
Outcry always, but rebellion, I didn't see it.
Where would we go?
No, the Germans were-- they let anybody know, if [INAUDIBLE] they will kill you, probably, right away.
So nothing happens that I didn't see that.
But.
More than the necessary--
Yeah, always.
They-- they tried to push as much as they could, the Germans.
They want to-- to finish with-- with the people.
No, they were pretty good at how much they can put extra, you know.
Always they would find a place to push them in.
I didn't see anything that-- they didn't have any other way, you know.
Where were they going to live?
Out?
For what?
They couldn't put it back in another.
Everything was very specific with their numbers, you know.
Oh, yeah, yeah.
They-- they knew how many.
And they knew how many extra could put them in.
No.
The only thing you hear is-- is the scratching on the walls.
That's what I hear myself.
They're scratching the walls.
They know-- they-- they know all the way.
I-- I think-- I don't know.
I don't know if it was a German, or the capos did most of the work.
You know.
They closed the door.
I mean, it's not too much.
And when you are there, you know, everybody's-- they don't wait too long, you know.
Well they were always ready upstairs to, you know, their signals, you know, you put them in.
It doesn't take too long, you know.
I mean the-- the Zyklon B, whatever, Zyklon B, when the gas gets with the air or something-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you're finished.
No, I-- I don't.
There was a procedure-- very, very, simple, I think.
They-- they knew it-- you do it once, twice, a few times, and you know that is a routine going on.
Because always it was from 1,500 to 2,500 people.
So they-- they knew exactly.
They never brought things except when really they-- they think it was necessary.
And that is when-- and they accommodate.
The undressing room?
[CHUCKLING] You had to pick up-- we had to pick up everything.
Everything goes to Germany or wherever it was, or.
The only thing they left was, I think, the-- the food.
I don't know.
I think they used to put it in trucks or whatever it was.
They didn't-- they were pretty organized.
I didn't do it, but they did it.
Yeah.
No.
But we hear a couple of times, you know, that there were somebody in there they didn't know.
It was a-- it was a very young child or something.
I think once or twice I heard some, because sometime, you know, after the-- there were-- the-- the gas came in.
And a couple of times we went in, we see maybe a very-- not me, but [?
Morris ?] once saw a very small guy that was alive after, you know, because probably the-- the mother wanted to catch up and-- and maybe he can save it.
But nothing happened.
The German came in, gave him a shot, and boom, I'm finished, you know.
They were very-- the Germans were very good at executing people.
No, no.
That was in the-- the gas chambers.
The undressing area, no.
Yeah.
And then the Germans would come in and kill him-- finished.
It was 30 minutes, because they-- they were waiting for us to come in.
The-- the ventilation should start.
And when it was OK for us to come in, they will open the door.
No, but I think Shlomo once was up there helping the SS.
I tried to stay out as much as I could from the-- from the eyes of the Germans.
I don't think I could survive.

Because my main game at that point, that I knew that my days were numbered, is to be able to get the-- not get the attention of the Germans, but for me to be in a-- in a way out of them, to be for the next day and the next day and the next day.
Like I told you before, you know, that war is going to end one day.
It couldn't be forever.
And when that ends, I'd like to be alive and be able to-- to tell the story, which I was happy to do.
Oh it's always successful because they just waited 30 minutes.
They knew that it takes from 2 to 5 minutes, depending where they are.
There's no-- and they're through, you know.
So they waited-- they way 30 minutes to be able to open the doors and put the ventilation on so we can go and do our job.
They were screaming yeah.
Yeah, they were.
A lot of crying, a lot of, you know-- could you imagine, you are with all your family there, and you know you are dying?
You know, it just doesn't take too long.
That's why if you knew somebody, we tell them, you go there, where it's two minutes.
In two minutes you are gone.
If not, you go to the five minutes, you are going to three minutes of suffering is a big deal.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Could you imagine that?
Now that I'm thinking to tell people you will die in three minutes or two minutes or five minutes.
Well, yeah.
When you are dead, well you're going to-- you are going to cry no more.
You are going to [INAUDIBLE] can tell anything.
And the process was, as far as I knew, that it would take from 2 to 5 minutes.
After, very seldom you would find somebody that was alive.
And ?] you never find somebody is alive.
Not me.
All these things, the capos are doing most of the things.
We were not getting involved too much in--
Yeah, we were busy doing something-- cleaning, you know, where there's a lot of people over there that left everything there.
So some of the food always we took out for ourselves.
How they-- how they died.
Yeah.
I [INAUDIBLE].
Tough, tough things.
Always the capos were.
That was a-- a very terrific scene.
You see all from regular people, you see just coming in a few minutes ago.
You open the door after 30 minutes, and you see a [INAUDIBLE] 1,500 2,000, 2,500, all dead.
This is always that my mind, because it is a very, very hard view to see people that you saw them before, and all of a sudden you see them 5, 10 minutes late, they are all dead.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you know, with the gas-- with the gas they get blue, and they get, you know, you have to work hard to untie them to be able to drag them out, put them in the thing, to put them in the elevator.
Well it looks, you know, they are like this, like that.
Always they are bending.
They stay up, you know, when the gas [INAUDIBLE] goes down.
I don't know, [INAUDIBLE] different things, the place of the body.
But we have to untangle everything.
We had to be powerful to get even-- if they were like this, together, you know, or [INAUDIBLE] were a few people.
We are not alone.
Not too many.
A few-- a few.
Four, five of them, I think.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Always were-- were people helping us.
They want to get rid fast, put them to the ovens so that we can paint it and finish with it.
So maybe another transport was coming, you know.
Maybe a-- we did a-- a cane, and a-- [INAUDIBLE] think, a strap or whatever.
But always [INAUDIBLE] things there to-- to work, and-- that [INAUDIBLE] take them to be ready to be-- to be go to the ovens.
[INAUDIBLE].
Put the cane up in here, [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah.
Well, the-- well, it put it-- put it in a stretcher, whatever it was, you know, to take it to the elevator.
But we had a few people taking one or two at a time to the elevator.
I don't know.
I-- I don't remember.
No, I think we were taking them out first.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And some, we didn't have to put in a stretcher.
We just move around to the elevator.
It was not too far away.
Not long.
It was just a-- a few minutes.
Is that mine?
Well, the only procedure is we take the bodies up to the elevator, to the second floor, and to the ovens.
And people are there doing their job, put-- putting-- we have nothing, anything to do when we are there.
So they would put it in a stretcher, put it inside the oven, and get cremated.
Every 20 minutes you-- they turn around, you know.
I think-- I think about four or six.
I don't know, four or six.
Four.
And after 20 minutes you turn them around, and 30 minutes.
And then you put more.
Well, we were just putting the corpse up to the elevator to the ovens.
And then the-- the other people there-- two, three of them-- they take over.
And we leave.
I knew their names.
I don't remember them now.
We were pretty good, the relationships, you know, about with us, because I think they were the ones-- that there were a couple of them, they were Germans or something, and-- [INAUDIBLE].
But most of the capos, they were with us in the number 2, crematorium 2, were pretty good.
No.
It's not.
We already-- when I was there, there was already capos.
No.
[CHUCKLING]
Yeah, well, there were, you know, in each crematorium, there were-- there were about two or three.
And there were general capo was one by the name of Kaminsky that was a-- was very good one.
And at they end, they kill him.
The-- the SS kill him.
Well, he never-- whenever the Germans asked him, he never revealed names or anything else, you know.
So he was a pretty good man.
But it didn't last at-- at the end.
After the revolution, I think, they didn't-- he-- the Germans were talking to him, but he never revealed any names, whoever was-- who was in the revolution, who did that with [INAUDIBLE].
And finally they put him in the oven himself.
We were-- we had only two SS.
One of them was from Holland, which is pretty-- was good enough.
And the other was a very bad man, you know, that I know [INAUDIBLE].
We didn't have any Germans to-- to do.
They were outside, sitting outside-- nothing to do.
But one was a terrible guy.
No.
No.
The other was from Holland.
I don't even remember his name.
Well, it was very bad behavior, you know.
He was not a nice man.
Anything that they find you, you did something, he would kill you, or-- or give you-- I don't know, just very bad man.
Who, me?
I-- I haven't seen him killing.
But it was his behavior-- everyone around knew that he wasn't a good man.
No.
Because, we didn't ever have nothing to do with him, you know.
He was outside sitting.
We had two people at the time, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Everything was done by the capos-- everything.
They were the people that we tried to be in a good name.
Which one?
They only one I know about that was Moll, I think-- Otto Moll.
Otto Moll was a very, very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sadistic man.

And he will-- when they were coming in, you know, they would pick up about 20 young girls, you know, and just undress them, and try to shoot them in the-- in the-- in the places, you know, what they call them-- just the breasts, and here.
So that was Moll.
Well, I was there.
I was seeing when he was coming and everything else.
I, you know, he was coming always 1, 1:30 in the morning.
And I would see the motorcycle coming in, and I knew that is Moll, you know.
I was there, you know.
I was-- I was working at the time.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He-- he-- I think I wrote a little bit about him, you know.
He was very, very sadistic man.
And I don't-- I don't know how he used to do that.

And the-- the other problem with this kind of people is after work, they used to go to their families not too far away, a couple of miles from-- from the camp, and having-- having a wife and children.
And I could-- I couldn't think or believe that a guy of this nature would do such things to-- to the children.
No, never.
No.
No.
Yeah, yeah.
Well he used to bring the people from-- from the station, you know, to the crematorium
In front of the people that are there.
Just-- very, very, very bad.
I don't even like to tell the story.
Yeah.
I always told the story about Moll-- Otto Moll.
Always.
I wrote about and everything.
It-- it was a very, very bad feelings.
All of the people that work in the sonderkommando, the feelings that you get in-- in your soul.
And then you don't know what to do.
Always-- I always, at night, many times, I will-- and the only thing I will think is Moll.
Because he is the-- the people that I observe.
And I don't know, and I don't-- don't think that were pretty nice things to do.
I don't care who you are in society.
And we were looking at there.
And they did so many things that were not-- you know, they were killing the people.
What do you have to play with them, and having taken young girls and shooting them [INAUDIBLE] here and here and there.
No-- responsibility, no.
I-- I know people, there-- there are a lot of people who are survivors, they don't care about us at all, you know.
But who-- we couldn't do anything else?
I think you have a few pieces that you saw of rabbis and things, probably, and [INAUDIBLE].
They say about, you know, what-- but I can do nothing anymore.
We had-- we had there about 10 Russian people.
They were in the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were in the Russian army.
And these are the ones.
And with one Greek guy, [INAUDIBLE] Baruch, that they put together, you know, a scheme how to be able to make a revolt in the sonderkommando.
This is what I know, that they, they were in Block 2 first, I think.
And they put the scheme.
And that Greek guy was a captain in the Greek army.
And they-- but we didn't-- they-- they put a date for the revolution, you know.
I think it was 19 of October.
I have all the-- and it didn't happen because the first one was because of the-- were the people on the-- on the thing, they were on the-- I don't know, I can't remember the name of the place.
They were-- they were outside-- the Germans were [INAUDIBLE] keeping the-- an eye on the-- on the-- on the camp, and they-- they couldn't make it.
Something happens, and we abandoned that date of revolution.
So they put a newer [INAUDIBLE].
But the only thing I-- I know, I think I-- you can read it.
You'll find out exactly what happened.
Because what they-- we saw is that 1:30 in the morning one day, we see flames coming from crematorium 3.
And we didn't know what it was.
But we were in crematorium 2 always ready to-- to be part of it.
And so-- but the-- the-- the Polish guy that were in our number 2 didn't let us do anything.
So we didn't do anything.
But they did theirs on 3 and 4.
And I think they burned number 4 crematorium.
And there were about the only thing we knew that in the morning, you know, they tried to-- to get out, and-- and maybe get rid of the camp and go.
And they couldn't do it, because the SS were there.
And they killed 600 of them.
And in the morning we had to put them in the oven ourselves.
That's the only thing I know.
Yeah.
Yeah, the people-- to the people that did the revolution and the other ones.
Well, I-- I felt that we wanted to be part of it.

I-- so I from there, I went to Rome myself, and I-- I from Rome I went to Greece, to Athens, where I worked for four years for the American Joint Distribution Committee at that time.
You know, at that time Athens was the biggest warehouse after the war where they used to come from all over Europe and go to places.
And so they were distributing all this the American Joint Distribution Committee was sending there.
Were distributing the-- all [INAUDIBLE] to get dress and everything else.
And from-- from there I-- after four years, they-- they finished the work.
And I was an Italian citizen, you know.
And I didn't think in Greece I couldn't get work at that time.
